category_title: Typical children who exhibit egocentric moral orientations focused on personal consequences
minimal_context: The student is participating in a one-on-one interview and answering questions about stealing in the Heinz Dilemma.
dialogue:
TUTOR: We just read the story about Heinz stealing the medicine. Why is it wrong to steal?

STUDENT: Because it’s not right to steal.

TUTOR: What else do you think about it?

STUDENT: You have to obey so you don't get punished. Like how a scout must obey his scoutmaster, because if he does not, the scoutmaster might make him do all the chores.

TUTOR: Can you give me another reason why it is wrong?

STUDENT: Because.

options.A: Review the dilemma and confirm comprehension by asking the student to relate what the story was about. If needed, review the question again before prompting a choice.
options.B: Immediately assign an article on school discipline policies, asking the student to read it before prompting a choice.
options.C: Tell the student, 'Don't say because, you can give a reason,' requiring them to answer before prompting a choice.
options.D: Use a poem aimed at promoting reflection and reinforcement of mindfulness concepts, asking the student to relate it before prompting a choice.
